data.title: Honeywell (HON): Profit Margin Decline Challenges Bullish Narratives on Growth and Valuation
data.description:
Honeywell International (HON) closed out the quarter with a net profit margin of 14.3%, slipping from last year’s 15.5% as annual earnings contracted despite a five-year annual earnings growth rate of 3.7%. Looking ahead, analysts expect earnings to grow at a 7.4% rate per year, well behind the US market’s 15.5% average, and revenue growth to track at 4.4% annually. With margins still healthy and a reputation for quality earnings, the results highlight a steady but unspectacular performance relative to broader market expectations.

See our full analysis for Honeywell International.

Next, we’ll put these earnings numbers head-to-head with the current market narrative to see whether the community’s expectations match the reality, or if there are surprises in the underlying story.

See what the community is saying about Honeywell International

Advertisement

Separation Costs Stretch Margin Recovery

  • Honeywell faces over $1.5 billion in one-time costs from its plan to separate into three public companies. These expenses may weigh down margin expansion efforts despite a recent net profit margin of 14.3%.
  • Bears argue these restructuring expenses and execution risks could pressure near-term earnings, even if profit margins are projected to climb to 16.9% in three years.
    • Expected separation costs, together with $500 million in possible tariff exposure, may counteract positive impacts from future margin improvement. This highlights their concern about short-term profitability.
    • Bears also point out that revenue growth forecasts of only 3.7% per year trail both the US market and consensus expectations, adding to skepticism over Honeywell’s plans to unlock value through its breakup.
    Bulls say execution hiccups could drag on results. See why they’re sounding the alarm. 🐻 Honeywell International Bear Case

Dividend Strength Stands Out in Sector

  • While Honeywell’s price-to-earnings ratio of 24.5x is well below the peer average of 28.7x, its combination of high-quality earnings and an attractive dividend helps differentiate it from industry rivals.
  • The consensus narrative notes that ongoing share repurchases and segment growth in areas such as LNG and data centers are set to support stable dividends and enhance long-term value.
    • Analysts expect the number of shares outstanding to decline by 2.36% annually over the next three years. This can further boost earnings per share and keep dividend growth on a steady trajectory.
    • Expansion into high-growth verticals and continued productivity improvements are positioned to reinforce Honeywell’s appeal for dividend-focused investors, offering resilience even as margin recovery takes time.

Valuation Trades at a Premium to Industrials

  • Despite offering better value than direct peers, Honeywell’s 24.5x earnings multiple is still nearly double the global industrials sector average of 12.7x. Its $220.67 share price remains above DCF fair value of $202.34.
  • According to the analysts’ consensus view, the share price is trailing the official price target of $249.40, but the gap between current price and sector multiples reflects a premium that demands delivery on margin guidance and strategic execution.
    • While the stock looks attractive versus peers, the premium against broader industrials and the modest growth trajectory raise the bar for future performance.
    • Any shortfall in executing the separation plan or realizing expected revenue gains could narrow that premium quickly, making valuation a central debate point for both bulls and bears.

data.title: Does MARA Holdings Offer Real Value After 9% Jump Fuelled by Digital Expansion?
data.description:
If you are standing at the crossroads wondering whether to buy, hold, or sell MARA Holdings stock, you are definitely not alone. The journey for MARA has been anything but dull, and the latest market moves have only added to the debate over what the company is really worth right now. Over the last month, shares have powered up by 9.0%, building on an 11.7% gain since the start of the year. While it’s true there was a slight dip of -1.8% in the past week, the stock’s upward march over the last five years—an increase of 789.8%—reminds us of the growth potential that can lurk beneath volatility.

Recent headlines have focused on MARA’s aggressive expansion into new digital infrastructure projects, bolstering optimism about its long-term outlook. Investors are also weighing management’s bold moves into global partnerships, which could reshape competitive dynamics for years to come. Although these developments add fresh intrigue, the big question really comes down to valuation: with so much already baked into the price, is MARA still undervalued?

Our valuation checks suggest there is still a lot to like. On our scorecard covering six major valuation criteria, MARA Holdings nails four, earning it a value score of 4. That is a strong showing, and it means the stock lines up as undervalued on most of the metrics that matter.

Next, let’s break down which valuation approaches support MARA, where it falls short, and explore a smarter way to judge whether this company truly deserves your investment dollars.

Why MARA Holdings is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: MARA Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works by estimating the future cash flows a company will generate and discounting those amounts back to today’s dollars. This gives investors a clear sense of what the business might really be worth, beyond just current market hype or volatility.

For MARA Holdings, analysts estimate last year's Free Cash Flow (FCF) at negative $1,706.7 Million, reflecting ambitious reinvestment and ongoing growth initiatives. Looking ahead, projections become more optimistic. By the end of 2026, FCF is forecast to reach $146.5 Million, and by 2035, simply extrapolated projections point to approximately $890.3 Million in annual FCF, all measured in US Dollars. While only the first five years are based on analysts' estimates, these longer-range numbers offer a sense of the potential trajectory if growth continues at similar rates.

Based on these DCF projections, MARA Holdings’ intrinsic value is calculated at $24.08 per share, implying the stock is trading at a 20.2% discount to its estimated fair value. This means that, despite some near-term volatility and sizable reinvestment costs, the current price still undervalues the company’s long-term cash-generating potential.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MARA Holdings.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests MARA Holdings is undervalued by 20.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: MARA Holdings Price vs Earnings

For companies that are profitable, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely trusted metric for valuation. It gives investors a direct sense of how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it especially relevant when a business is generating positive net income like MARA Holdings.

A company’s PE ratio is shaped by growth expectations and risk. Fast-growing and lower-risk firms usually command higher PE ratios, while those with uncertain outlooks or industry headwinds tend to trade at lower multiples. This context makes it key to compare MARA’s PE with industry standards and similar companies.

MARA Holdings currently trades at a PE of 10.5x, which is significantly below both the software industry average of 33.6x and its direct peers at 64.0x. At first glance, this discount might look like a bargain. However, peer and industry multiples can be skewed by outliers or companies with different growth or risk profiles. A higher or lower PE does not always signal mispricing.

To get a clearer picture, Simply Wall St uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio” model that factors in elements like MARA’s earnings growth, margins, market cap, industry norms, and risk profile to set a more tailored benchmark. For MARA Holdings, the Fair Ratio is 2.6x, much lower than its current 10.5x. This means MARA is trading above the level justified by our analysis of its fundamentals.

Result: OVERVALUED

PE ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your MARA Holdings Narrative

Earlier, we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives, a modern, hands-on tool for smarter investing. A Narrative is simply your story about a company, tying your outlook and assumptions (like future revenue, margins, and fair value estimates) directly to a financial forecast and then to a calculated fair value. Narratives bridge the gap between the numbers and the real-world events or beliefs driving them, helping you see exactly how your opinions play out in a company’s price tag.

Available right on Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives empower millions of investors to map their perspective to a fair value and compare it to the latest share price. In addition, Narratives automatically update as new information comes in, from earnings to news, ensuring your analysis stays current. This gives investors a practical edge because by simply comparing Fair Value to Price, you can decide when to buy, hold, or sell based on your unique view.

For example, some users see MARA Holdings unlocking new recurring revenue through AI and renewable energy expansion, setting a fair value as high as $28.00, while others remain cautious about its bitcoin mining risks and estimate fair value closer to $18.00. These diverging Narratives show how interpreting the same company through your own lens can lead to very different investment decisions.

To believe in Ivanhoe Electric as a shareholder right now, you have to trust in the vision for North American copper and critical mineral development, and have confidence that early exploration work will eventually justify current valuations, despite the company’s ongoing losses and limited revenue. The latest US$150 million equity raise is a clear signal that management is prioritizing growth at key projects like the Santa Cruz Copper Project, with the capital largely earmarked to secure land and accelerate development. data.title: SouthState (SSB) Earnings Jump 39.8%, Reinforcing Bullish Margin Narratives Despite Peer Valuation Premium

Looking ahead, earnings are forecast to rise at 17.8% per year, outpacing the US market’s projected 15.5% annual growth. Revenue is set to trail the national pace at 8.8% growth per year versus the broader forecast of 10%. With profits climbing and net margins holding strong, investors have reasons to be optimistic about the bank’s renewed momentum.

    • This focus on operational efficiency is supported by recent acquisitions and scale. This scale enables investments in technology and digital platforms that support higher margins over time.
    • Low credit costs and low volatility in asset quality suggest SouthState can withstand regulatory and competitive pressures while still supporting margin growth.

What stands out is how closely the results and forecast improvements reinforce the consensus narrative that margin durability is a distinguishing strength for SouthState. data.description:
If you’re sitting on the fence, wondering what to do about Bayerische Motoren Werke stock, you’re not alone. Investors have watched BMW’s price edge up 1.0% in the past week, even as the broader auto sector faces big questions, and after a modest dip of -3.9% over the last month. And the five-year picture? That’s a hefty 84.4% gain, which is not too shabby in a competitive market.

What’s driving all this? Part of the recent narrative includes rising interest in electrified vehicle strategies and the ongoing buzz around premium car demand in Europe and Asia. Supply chains have stabilized, helping to calm some nerves. Meanwhile, global headlines about regulatory policy shifts and sustainability targets continue to swirl, adding new dimensions to the way investors assess risk and upside here. It all stacks up to an environment where people are actively weighing not just where BMW has been, but where it could go next.

Curious about whether BMW is undervalued, fairly priced, or maybe even a bargain? According to our current valuation checks, BMW gets a value score of 4 out of 6, indicating the company is undervalued in most but not all of the benchmarks analysts watch closely. Up next, we’ll break down exactly what those valuation approaches are, how BMW stacks up on each, and, as promised, introduce an even better way to gauge true value at the end of this article.

Bayerische Motoren Werke delivered 10.9% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Auto industry.

Approach 1: Bayerische Motoren Werke Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s value by forecasting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to their present value. This approach aims to reveal what the company’s operations are truly worth, based on how much cash they are expected to generate over time.

For Bayerische Motoren Werke, the most recent reported Free Cash Flow (FCF) stands at negative €1.19 billion, a figure reflecting recent investments or fluctuating working capital. Analysts project strong recovery and growth, with FCF expected to reach €5.42 billion by 2029. Over the next decade, Simply Wall St extrapolates these trends further and shows annual FCF hovering around €5.2 billion by 2035. Most of these estimates rely on a blend of analyst expectations for the next five years and algorithmic projections for the years beyond.

Based on these projections, the DCF model calculates an intrinsic value of €95.99 per share. With the stock currently trading at a price approximately 16.3% below this estimate, the model indicates that BMW is significantly undervalued relative to its true long-term cash-generating potential.

Approach 2: Bayerische Motoren Werke Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a classic and practical tool for valuing profitable companies like Bayerische Motoren Werke. By comparing the stock price to its earnings, investors get a quick sense of whether the market expects continued growth or sees added risk. Generally, higher growth expectations or lower perceived risks justify a higher "normal" or "fair" PE ratio. In contrast, slower growth or increased risks lead to lower multiples being seen as reasonable.

BMW is currently trading at a PE ratio of 8.6x. For context, this sits below the auto industry average of 18.8x and also undercuts the average for its peer companies, which is 10.9x. This initial look suggests that the market may be undervaluing BMW relative to its sector.

data.title: Institutional investors have a lot riding on Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited (LON:SREI) with 48% ownership
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Given the large stake in the stock by institutions, Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust's stock price might be vulnerable to their trading decisions
  • 51% of the business is held by the top 10 shareholders
  • Using data from company's past performance alongside ownership research, one can better assess the future performance of a company

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

A look at the shareholders of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited (LON:SREI) can tell us which group is most powerful. With 48% stake, institutions possess the maximum shares in the company. Put another way, the group faces the maximum upside potential (or downside risk).

Last week’s 15% gain means that institutional investors were on the positive end of the spectrum even as the company has shown strong longer-term trends. One-year return to shareholders is currently 16% and last week’s gain was the icing on the cake.

In the chart below, we zoom in on the different ownership groups of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust.

View our latest analysis for Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

We can see that Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust does have institutional investors; and they hold a good portion of the company's stock. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. It is not uncommon to see a big share price drop if two large institutional investors try to sell out of a stock at the same time. So it is worth checking the past earnings trajectory of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust, (below). Of course, keep in mind that there are other factors to consider, too.

Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust is not owned by hedge funds. Our data shows that Schroder Investment Management Limited is the largest shareholder with 12% of shares outstanding. In comparison, the second and third largest shareholders hold about 9.9% and 8.5% of the stock.

On further inspection, we found that more than half the company's shares are owned by the top 10 shareholders, suggesting that the interests of the larger shareholders are balanced out to an extent by the smaller ones.

Researching institutional ownership is a good way to gauge and filter a stock's expected performance. The same can be achieved by studying analyst sentiments. As far as we can tell there isn't analyst coverage of the company, so it is probably flying under the radar.

Insider Ownership Of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust

The definition of company insiders can be subjective and does vary between jurisdictions. Our data reflects individual insiders, capturing board members at the very least. Management ultimately answers to the board. However, it is not uncommon for managers to be executive board members, especially if they are a founder or the CEO.

Insider ownership is positive when it signals leadership are thinking like the true owners of the company. However, high insider ownership can also give immense power to a small group within the company. This can be negative in some circumstances.

Our most recent data indicates that insiders own less than 1% of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited. It has a market capitalization of just UK£270m, and the board has only UK£184k worth of shares in their own names. Many investors in smaller companies prefer to see the board more heavily invested. You can click here to see if those insiders have been buying or selling.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 45% stake in Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust. This size of ownership, while considerable, may not be enough to change company policy if the decision is not in sync with other large shareholders.

Public Company Ownership

It appears to us that public companies own 7.0% of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust. It's hard to say for sure but this suggests they have entwined business interests. This might be a strategic stake, so it's worth watching this space for changes in ownership.

Next Steps:

While it is well worth considering the different groups that own a company, there are other factors that are even more important. Take risks for example - Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust has 3 warning signs (and 1 which is a bit unpleasant) we think you should know about.
